Title: Sister, Sister - Score, Score
Subtitle: Grace and Mary Wilkowski help turn St. Andrew's into a high-scoring unit.
Author: Erin Rossiter
Summary: A Savannah Morning News feature on Grace and Mary Wilkowski, their fast-paced 2003-04 St. Andrew's team, and the sisters' role in the Saints' return to the top of SCISA Class AA.

Images.alt: Page 1 of the January 23, 2004 Savannah Morning News feature on Grace and Mary Wilkowski
Images.caption: Sister, Sister - Score, Score, page 1

Images.alt: Page 2 of the January 23, 2004 Savannah Morning News feature on Grace and Mary Wilkowski
Images.caption: Sister, Sister - Score, Score, page 2

Transcript:
SISTER, SISTER - SCORE, SCORE
GRACE AND MARY WILKOWSKI HELP TURN ST. ANDREW'S INTO A HIGH-SCORING UNIT.

You might not recognize the game St. Andrew's plays, but the sisters on the basketball court should get your attention.

One quieter than the other, one feeding passes to the next, both aiming their team at a return to the top of SCISA Class AA, Grace and Mary Wilkowski are again dominant.

With eye-popping numbers, the Wilkowskis have helped lead their 9-3 Wilmington Island team to the head of SCISA Region 2-AA with a 5-0 mark heading into tonight's game against Colleton Prep.

But they are doing it in a way no one may have expected.

The team's focus this year is on scoring, whereas last year the Saints focused on controlled possessions, selective shots and conservative dig-in defense.

"Last year, our goals were to play well and to have a limited number of turnovers," said Mary, a 5-foot-11 freshman. "If the score was two to nothing at the end it didn't matter, as long as you come out on the upper side."

The Saints did come out on the upper side, winning the SCISA Class AA title by beating Greenville's Carolina Academy 47-42.

Their transition into a fastbreak offense happened with a few staff changes over the summer, including new coach Kevin Kunst and new point guard Jennifer Moesch, who transferred from St. Vincent's.

"Our other guards are very reliable and consistent but she adds the dimension of spontaneity when she scores," Grace said. "She is very unpredictable for the other team as far as when she is going to take a dive to the basket."

Kunst, who coaches the Saints' football team, asked school administrators if he could lead the team midsummer after former coach and athletic director Deb Morrell took a job at Johnson High.

Some of the first changes Kunst made were adding players and changing their offensive style to suit the talents of the smaller players, like 5-foot-9 Grace, as well as the fleet-footed Moesch.

Then, he challenged them to be more offensive-minded.

"Sixty percent from the free-throw line, 60 points," he said, repeating his instructions. "If they can do that, we can beat anybody."

With only six players on the varsity team last year, the Saints were very careful with their shots and stayed conservative on defense.

They scored more than 60 points four times during their 2002-2003 championship season. This year's group, which lost only senior Meghan Lowe, has already eclipsed that number with 10 games left to play.

And the Wilkowskis are leading the way.

Grace, a sophomore, is averaging 17.6 points and 13.3 rebounds a game. In her last three games, she scored 29, 22 and 29 points.

"It's like watching a ballet," Kunst said. "She never makes a mistake. She never gets flustered. Her experience almost never changes the whole game."

Her sister, Mary, is boisterous and vocal on the court. Grace described her as reliable, aggressive and a "dominating presence" in a game.

Their work as a pair on the court is also evident.

The girls have played basketball together since their childhood days in Charlottesville, Va. Less than one year apart in age, they graduated to organized ball at the YMCA in elementary school before moving to Savannah.

"We complement each other really well on the court," Grace said. "We play really well together."

Their teammates know it.

"The girls who are playing with Mary and Grace, they know they are playing with something special," Kunst said.

They also know what's coming next year.

Another sister.

St. Andrew's eighth-grader Rose Wilkowski is slated to advance to the varsity next season.

"All three will probably start next year, which will be interesting," Kunst said.

THE WILKOWSKIS' FILE

Who: Grace, 5-foot-9 sophomore
School: St. Andrew's
Statistics: 17.6 ppg, 13.3 rpg

Who: Mary, 5-11 freshman
School: St. Andrew's
Statistics: 13.5 ppg, 12.2 rpg
